stories.title: Greystar Sells Virginia Apartment Tower for $216M
stories.excerpt: Greystar, perhaps the largest multifamily landlord in the U.S., has unloaded another property, this time in Northern Virginia. Investment firm GID put down $215.8 million to acquire the 423-unit luxury apartment tower in Arlington, Va., and changed its name to Windsor Courthouse. The deal for the 20-story development at 2055 15th Street North is equal to more than $510,000 per unit. The Business Journals reported the transaction, citing data from CoStar. Berkadia arranged the transaction on behalf of Greystar. Neither Boston-based GID nor Charleston, S.C.-based Greystar responded to requests for comment for this article. Located next to the Court House Metro station, Windsor Courthouse was completed in 2023, and it is the tallest residential building in Arlington’s Court House neighborhood. It features 18,500 square feet of ground-floor retail leased to tenants such as French café Maman, YogaSix and Playa Bowls. Japanese restaurant Saki is set to open in another spot at Windsor Courthouse soon. Berkadia’s Brian Crivella, Yalda Ghamarian, Bill Gribbin and Jack Canepa handled the deal for Greystar. The acquisition expands GID’s portfolio in Northern Virginia, managed by its Windsor Communities subsidiary, which runs Halstead Tower by Windsor in Alexandria, Io Piazza by Windsor in Arlington and the Ridgewood by Windsor in Fairfax. Greystar also maintains a presence in the submarket after completing a 231-unit high-rise across the street last year. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Greystar sells Arlington, VA luxury tower (423 units, 2023-built) to GID for $215.8M ($510K/unit).
Windsor Courthouse adjacent to Court House Metro; 18.5K SF retail (Maman, YogaSix, Playa Bowls, Saki).
Greystar portfolio rationalization signals capital redeployment; buyer discipline at $510K/unit in Arlington submarkets.
stories.summary: Greystar offloaded Windsor Courthouse, a 423-unit luxury tower completed in 2023 in Arlington, Virginia, to investment firm GID for $215.8 million ($510,000 per unit). The 20-story building sits adjacent to Court House Metro station and includes 18,500 square feet of ground-floor retail. Berkadia arranged the transaction. The deal signals Greystar's continued portfolio rotation and suggests cap-rate or return expectations may be shifting in Northern Virginia.
stories.impact: Greystar's willingness to sell newly-stabilized assets in Arlington—a prime transit corridor—suggests either reached return targets or anticipated market softening. Watch for whether recent vintage suburban DC apartments are trading at prices that make ground-up development less attractive, or if Greystar is rotating capital to higher-growth or value-add opportunities elsewhere. Metro-adjacent suburban DC multifamily may be reaching maturity-phase pricing.

stories.title: KLNB Represents Chinese Restaurant Concept in National Harbor Lease
stories.excerpt: KLNB announced that restaurant brand Nan Xiang Xiao Long Bao has opened its new location at 180 American Way in National Harbor. KLNB Principal Josh Weiner represented Nan Xiang Xiao Long Bao in securing the lease, in collaboration with Jerry Johnson of Bennett Williams Retail, who served as co-broker and master broker for the tenant. Tony Summers represented the landlord, Peterson Companies. The Michelin-recommended restaurant signed the lease in 2024 for the 6,253-square-foot space. The restaurant serves Northern Chinese cuisine, including signature soup dumplings and features a full bar service. “We began by evaluating the market based on Nan Xiang’s specific expansion criteria and toured numerous opportunities throughout the region,” said KLNB’s Josh Weiner. “We’re excited to help bring Nan Xiang to National Harbor and introduce this acclaimed concept to even more customers throughout the Washington region.” The post KLNB Represents Chinese Restaurant Concept in National Harbor Lease appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Nan Xiang Xiao Long Bao opens at National Harbor; 6,253-SF lease signed 2024, Michelin-recommended.
Peterson Companies/Bennett Williams retail brokerage; anchor tenant signals experiential retail demand.
National Harbor tenanting activity reflects DC metro demographic strength and mixed-use appeal.
stories.summary: Michelin-recommended restaurant Nan Xiang Xiao Long Bao signed a 6,253-square-foot lease at 180 American Way in National Harbor, Maryland, leased in 2024. KLNB and Bennett Williams facilitated the transaction with Peterson Companies as landlord. The concept serves Northern Chinese cuisine with full bar service.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance. However, National Harbor's continued success landing high-quality experiential retail tenants underscores the mixed-use district's resilience and appeal as a regional destination, which supports rental demand for nearby residential (apartments, townhomes) and justifies premium positioning in the broader DC suburban market.

stories.title: Southern California Retail Investment Jumps 62% in First Half of 2026
stories.excerpt: Even though less retail space is changing hands in Southern California, commercial real estate investment firms are still pouring a lot more into the asset class so far in 2026. Thanks to slow construction and improving sentiment, retail investment sales hit $3.52 billion during the first six months of the year in Los Angeles, Orange and Ventura counties and in California’s Inland Empire. That marks an almost 62 percent increase compared to this time last year, according to a new report from NAI Capital. That jump comes despite a 28 percent drop in the square footage traded over the first half of the year. About 8.35 million square feet in the aforementioned regions sold to new owners by the end of June, compared to 11.55 million square feet in the first half of 2025. NAI Capital reported deals have shifted to higher-value properties, and attributed the jump in investment to buyers and sellers being more aligned on pricing. Completed construction declined 45 percent annually to just 473,008 square feet, while the development pipeline also declined 10.2 percent to 1.5 million square feet. L.A. County led in volume with $1.7 billion in retail property sales, which is up 64.6 percent over last year, across nearly 4 million square feet, according to NAI Capital. Asking rents rose 1 percent to $2.98 per square foot triple net. Propelled by a 3.8 percent vacancy rate, Orange County’s retail investment sales hit $976 million. NAI Capital said demand remained particularly strong for smaller, premium retail properties and coastal assets. The Inland Empire also saw a relative investment spike with a 142 percent yearly increase, while its Riverside and San Bernardino counties recorded a 9.3 percent increase in leasing so far this year. For the region, though, leasing volume declined 14.6 percent compared to last year to 6.23 million square feet. But overall retail vacancy improved to 5.9 percent from 6.3 percent, and average asking rents increased 1.1 percent to $2.36 per squa
bullets: Southern CA retail investment hits $3.52B (H1 2026), +62% YoY; only 8.35M SF traded (-28% SF).
Completed retail construction fell 45% to 473K SF; development pipeline also declining.
Price appreciation on fewer, higher-value deals; buyers/sellers aligning on valuations after reset.
stories.summary: Southern California retail investment sales jumped 62% year-over-year to $3.52 billion in the first half of 2026 across Los Angeles, Orange, Ventura counties, and the Inland Empire, despite a 28% drop in square footage traded (8.35M SF vs. 11.55M SF in H1 2025). Completed retail construction plummeted 45% annually to 473,008 square feet. NAI Capital attributed the dollar increase to a shift toward higher-value properties and improved price alignment between buyers and sellers.
stories.impact: Tight retail supply and scarcity-driven pricing in Southern California signal limited new retail delivery, which elevates the value and desirability of mixed-use residential projects with ground-floor retail components. Developers pursuing dense, urban multifamily (e.g., Wellpointe's Viva L.A. concept) gain competitive advantage by bundling retail, which will command premium pricing. Ground-floor retail lease rates likely rising; bake higher rent assumptions into mixed-use underwriting for LA, Orange County, and Inland Empire infill projects.

stories.title: Bradford Allen Closes On 25K SF of Leases with Illinois Retina
stories.excerpt: Bradford Allen has executed two recent leases with Illinois Retina in the Chicago market. The medical services provider signed a 16,228-square-foot lease in Skokie, Illinois and an 8,728-square-foot lease in Munster, Indiana, 30 miles southeast of Chicago. Craig Nadborne, executive managing director, brokerage services at Bradford Allen, negotiated the leases on behalf of Illinois Retina. “For more than two decades, Illinois Retina has trusted us to help align its real estate with the needs of its physicians, staff and patients,” said Nadborne. “Relationships of that duration are built over time, and these latest transactions reflect the group’s growth and continued commitment to accessible, well-located care in the Chicago-area market.” The Skokie office is located at Concourse Office Plaza, 4709-4711 Golf Road. The long-term transaction represented a renewal and expansion. The property comprises two 12-story medical office buildings. Illinois Retina’s Munster location relocated from a smaller facility to 9200 Calumet Ave. as part of a long-term lease. The post Bradford Allen Closes On 25K SF of Leases with Illinois Retina appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Illinois Retina leases 16.2K SF in Skokie renewal/expansion; 8.7K SF relocation in Munster, IN.
Medical office expansion signal; healthcare services consolidating and upgrading space in Chicago metro.
Long-term leases reflect sector confidence in Chicago-area care delivery and practitioner retention.
stories.summary: Illinois Retina signed two leases totaling 25K square feet in the Chicago area: a 16,228-SF renewal and expansion in Skokie (Concourse Office Plaza) and an 8,728-SF relocation to Munster, Indiana. Both transactions reflect the medical provider's growth and long-term confidence in the region.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily impact. However, healthcare provider expansion and facility upgrades in suburban Chicago (Skokie, Munster) signal employment and worker population growth in those submarkets, which supports rental demand for apartments near medical office clusters. Watch for further medical/life-sciences anchoring in outlying Chicago suburbs, which can justify new residential development and support rent growth.

stories.title: Fort Worth Stockyards will get $71M apartment project as $1B planned redevelopment hangs in balance
stories.excerpt: Oldham Goodwin Group and Fort Worth Housing Solutions broke ground on The Exchange, a $71 million, 296-unit affordable apartment complex north of Fort Worth’s historic Stockyards. The apartments are a small slice of a 14.6-acre mixed-use development from Oldham Goodwin aimed at enhancing the Stockyards. The apartment complex features affordable units in partnership with Fort Worth Housing Solutions, and is slated to be the residential portion of the wider development. Amenities include a pool, clubhouse and a dog park. The apartment sizes begin at studio sizes and scale to three-bedroom units, according to the Dallas Business Journal.  Stockyards North, which […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: The Exchange: $71M, 296-unit affordable, broke ground; Oldham Goodwin + Fort Worth Housing Solutions.
Stockyards North 14.6-acre mixed-use; hotel, retail, dining; first residential phase of larger redevelopment.
Larger $1B Stockyards renovation mired in legal dispute; affordable housing fills void as anchor.
stories.summary: Oldham Goodwin Group and Fort Worth Housing Solutions broke ground on The Exchange, a $71 million, 296-unit affordable apartment complex north of Fort Worth's historic Stockyards. The development is the residential anchor for Stockyards North, a 14.6-acre mixed-use project that will also include a Home2Suites hotel, retail, and dining. A wider $1 billion second-phase renovation of the Stockyards remains stalled in legal disputes.
stories.impact: The Exchange's groundbreak signals confidence in the Fort Worth Stockyards submarket despite broader redevelopment litigation. Affordable housing at scale (296 units, $71M) positions developer for historic tax credit and public subsidy capture. For other developers: the Stockyards area remains a mixed-use destination play with strong experiential/tourism appeal; the legal delay on Phase 2 may actually benefit Phase 1 absorption by reducing supply competition. Watch for additional residential projects targeting the Stockyards North corridor as Phase 1 builds momentum.

stories.title: NYC Council Approves Monitor Point and Six Other Housing Developments
stories.excerpt: The New York City Council on Thursday approved rezoning plans poised to generate up to 3,250 homes across the five boroughs. City lawmakers voted in favor of the Gotham Organization’s Monitor Point rezoning application in Greenpoint, Brooklyn; Proto Property Services’ development at 2950 West 24th Street in Coney Island, Brooklyn; and Friedland Properties and the Chapman Group’s Dewitt Clinton Park North project in Manhattan’s Hell’s Kitchen. Monitor Point will have 1,324 total units, of which 662 will be affordable housing after City Council committees negotiated for 50 percent affordability rather than 40 percent. The project will also provide a new location for the Greenpoint Monitor Museum and an acre of green space. As recently as April 2025, however, the Gotham Organization had planned to provide 25 percent affordable housing at the site. “I feel very strongly that when we’re proposing developments on public land, those projects have to be primarily for the public good,” Brooklyn Councilmember Lincoln Restler said in a press conference before the vote. “We were able to get the developer to commit to building out over 50,000 square feet of open space, and the developer committing $300,000 per year to maintain Bushwick Inlet Park.” Under a ​​99-year ground lease with the Metropolitan Transportation Authority, which had a wash facility at the location, Gotham Organization will build the housing across two separate towers at 40 Quay Street. Restler said that his time negotiating with the developers goes back to the administration of former Mayor Bill de Blasio, who contacted him about being the public face of the public-private partnership sometime between winning his first election to the seat and being sworn in. The Gotham Organization did not immediately provide a statement. Meanwhile, Proto is seeking to build an 18-story mixed-use building with 408 units on West 24th Street in Coney Island, all of which will be specialized housing. Dewitt Clinton Park N
bullets: NYC Council approves 7 projects, 3,250 total homes: Monitor Point (1,324 units, 50% affordable in Greenpoint).
Monitor Point rezoning negotiated from 40% to 50% affordability; Gotham Organization obligated public-land contribution.
Greenpoint, Coney Island, Hell's Kitchen rezonings unlock supply pipeline; mixed affordability mix across boroughs.
stories.summary: The New York City Council approved seven housing development projects generating up to 3,250 homes across the five boroughs. Gotham Organization's Monitor Point in Greenpoint, Brooklyn, will deliver 1,324 units (662 affordable at 50%, up from originally proposed 40%), plus a new Greenpoint Monitor Museum location and one acre of green space. Proto Property Services and Friedland/Chapman Group projects in Coney Island and Hell's Kitchen were also approved.
stories.impact: NYC's rezoning wins—especially the 50% affordability claw-back at Monitor Point—signal tighter regulatory scrutiny on public-land deals and rising political pressure to maximize affordable units. For developers: expect routine affordability requirement negotiation and public benefit expectations to intensify. However, the 3,250-unit pipeline unlock is material for market supply and should ease some rental pressure in outer boroughs (Greenpoint, Coney Island) and Hell's Kitchen, depressing near-term rent growth in those submarkets while stabilizing longer-term fundamentals.

stories.title: JV of Synergy, Axonic Acquires Downtown Boston Office Tower
stories.excerpt: Locally based Synergy and New York-based Axonic Capital have acquired 265 Franklin St., a 21-story Class A office tower in Downtown Boston. The acquisition marks the first joint venture between the two firms. The seller was Clarendon Properties and the acquisition price was $116 million, according to the Boston Business Journal. “265 Franklin St. is an exceptional asset located at one of the most important intersections in Downtown Boston,” said David Greaney, Founder and CEO of Synergy. “Its institutional-quality construction, flexible floor plates, extensive recent capital improvements, and premier location create a compelling opportunity to continue enhancing the tenant experience while benefiting from the ongoing recovery and evolution of Boston’s office market.” Situated on a full city block bounded by Franklin, High, Oliver, and Batterymarch streets, 265 Franklin Street encompasses approximately 358,406 rentable square feet. The property offers immediate access to South Station, major MBTA transit lines, Interstate 90 and Interstate 93, as well as numerous dining, retail, and hospitality amenities. The post JV of Synergy, Axonic Acquires Downtown Boston Office Tower appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Synergy + Axonic JV acquires 265 Franklin St., Boston Class A office tower, $116M from Clarendon Properties.
21-story, 358K SF; transit-rich location (South Station, MBTA, I-90/93); recent capital improvements.
Office repositioning by local/regional capital; institutional-quality downtown Boston asset commands buying interest.
stories.summary: Synergy and Axonic Capital formed their first joint venture to acquire 265 Franklin Street, a 21-story Class A office tower in Downtown Boston, from Clarendon Properties for $116 million. The 358,406-rentable-square-foot property benefits from immediate access to South Station, MBTA transit, and I-90/I-93, with extensive recent capital improvements and flexible floor plates.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. However, Synergy's entrance into a JV and confidence in Boston office recovery signal that institutional capital sees downtown revitalization. For mixed-use developers: downtown Boston office stabilization may unlock ground-floor retail and mixed-use opportunity in the area. Monitor whether Boston office recovery draws tech/professional-services job growth that supports residential demand in adjacent Back Bay and Seaport submarkets.

stories.title: Cybersecurity Firm PKI Solutions Opens New Downtown Portland Office
stories.excerpt: PKI Solutions has relocated its corporate headquarters to downtown Portland, occupying a larger space at 1000 SW Broadway. The company cited gaining new customers and users, as well as expanding its team, as factors in moving to the new space. PKI Solutions provides software consulting, training, professional services, and assessments to help ensure the security of organizations. “We are thrilled to be moving into 1000 SW Broadway”, said Mark B. Cooper, Founder and CEO of PKI Solutions. “This new space not only gives us room to grow our team for the long-term, but it also reinforces our commitment to returning tech companies to the downtown area.” PKI Solutions is a member of the Technology Association of Oregon, and the move aligns with the association’s mission to support local tech businesses. “PKI Solutions’ move to Downtown Portland is exactly the kind of investment we want to see more of across our region”, said Skip Newberry, President & CEO at the Technology Association of Oregon. The post Cybersecurity Firm PKI Solutions Opens New Downtown Portland Office appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: PKI Solutions (cybersecurity) relocates HQ to 1000 SW Broadway, Downtown Portland; cited growth & recruitment.
Tech company downtown move reverses pandemic exodus; signals jobs returning to core city.
Downtown Portland office absorption and employer stability supports residential demand recovery.
stories.summary: Cybersecurity software firm PKI Solutions relocated its corporate headquarters to Downtown Portland at 1000 SW Broadway, citing customer growth, team expansion, and commitment to revitalizing the tech presence downtown. The company is a member of the Technology Association of Oregon and the move aligns with regional efforts to support local tech businesses.
stories.impact: PKI Solutions' downtown relocation is a rare bright spot in Portland's pandemic-era office and retail struggles. The move signals return of tech employment to core downtown, which supports renewed residential demand and justifies development of apartments, condos, or mixed-use projects in downtown Portland and inner east side neighborhoods. Watch for additional small-to-mid-market tech and professional-services relocations to downtown as a leading indicator of Portland's recovery trajectory and multifamily absorption potential.

stories.title: Wellpointe Plans $2B Affordable Senior Living Complex in Warner Center
stories.excerpt: Wellpointe Inc. unveiled plans for Viva L.A. at Warner Center, a four-phase, 100% affordable senior housing community at 6400 Canoga Ave. in Warner Center’s downtown district. The development represents a total estimated investment of $2 billion at the 4.71-acre Woodland Hills site that a Wellpointe property-holding affiliate acquired in 2025 from Parkview Financial. Designed by Gensler, Viva L.A. at Warner Center will comprise four high-rise towers ranging from 34 to 42 stories and will be located down the street from the planned Rams Village. The approximately 2.2-million-square-foot project, with 61,450 square feet of non-residential uses, will replace an existing commercial office building with 3,192 new affordable residential units. “Viva represents our conviction that affordable housing, paired with assisted living services as needed, can be delivered at the scale and density that California’s aging population in core urban centers actually needs,” said George Kutnerian, co-founder & CEO of Wellpointe. “Despite the overwhelming need for a new model, senior living continues to be built out rather than up – even in dense urban areas – and quality is treated as incompatible with affordability. Viva rejects that premise.” Where the apartment industry connects. Meet the owners, investors, developers, lenders, brokers, and service providers driving today’s multifamily market at Connect Apartments. Build relationships, uncover opportunities, and stay ahead of what’s next. The post Wellpointe Plans $2B Affordable Senior Living Complex in Warner Center appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Wellpointe plans Viva L.A. at Warner Center: $2B, 3,192 affordable senior units, 4 towers (34-42 stories).
2.2M SF on 4.71 acres; Gensler design; 100% affordable senior housing with assisted living services.
Senior housing density at scale; rejects sprawl model; positioned near Rams Village mixed-use anchor.
stories.summary: Wellpointe Inc. unveiled Viva L.A. at Warner Center, a $2 billion, four-phase senior housing development at 6400 Canoga Avenue in Woodland Hills. The project comprises four high-rise towers (34–42 stories) totaling 2.2 million square feet with 3,192 100% affordable residential units (plus 61,450 SF non-residential space) on a 4.71-acre site. Wellpointe acquired the property in 2025 from Parkview Financial and positioned the project near the planned Rams Village.
stories.impact: Viva L.A. exemplifies high-density, urban-infill senior housing model that diverges from traditional suburban sprawl, signaling investor thesis that California's aging population requires dense, transit-adjacent, service-rich environments. The $2B capital commitment and 100% affordability (likely via low-income housing tax credits, state funding, and senior housing subsidies) validates senior housing as institutional-quality asset class. For multifamily developers: watch senior housing subsidy and regulatory incentives expand in California; mixed-income/mixed-age infill development may benefit from synergies with senior components (shared amenities, workforce stability).

stories.title: Brookfield ups market-rate unit count, building heights at Pier 70 project
stories.excerpt: Brookfield Properties wants to pack hundreds more homes into its long-planned Pier 70 redevelopment in the Dogpatch.  The New York-based developer is seeking approval to increase the maximum residential count at the 28-acre Dogpatch waterfront site by roughly 28 percent from 2,150 units to 2,750, the San Francisco Chronicle reported. The proposal would also raise the height of the residential buildings from 70 feet to 90 feet, the maximum height allowed under the project’s existing voter-approved framework from 2015. The changes are aimed at reviving a project that has been stalled alongside much of the city’s market-rate housing pipeline. Brookfield […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Brookfield Pier 70 (Dogpatch, SF): seeking approval to raise units 28% (2,150 → 2,750), height 70 → 90 feet.
First market-rate phase: 700 units (up from 575); additional 70 units improve project underwriting economics.
Density trade-off for breakeven; construction/operating costs rising; SF inclusionary requirement reduced 15% → 5%.
stories.summary: Brookfield Properties is seeking approval to increase the residential unit count at its Pier 70 redevelopment in San Francisco's Dogpatch by 28% (from 2,150 to 2,750 units) and raise building heights from 70 to 90 feet (the voter-approved maximum). The additional density is aimed at enabling the first market-rate phase (350 units, rising to ~420) to pencil. Pier 70's first phase will now comprise 700 homes, up from 575. The developer cited rising construction and operating costs and San Francisco's reduced inclusionary housing requirements (from 15% to 5%) as drivers of the need for density.
stories.impact: Brookfield's request signals market-rate multifamily underwriting pressure in San Francisco: even after recent rent recovery, developers need to push density and height to achieve target returns given cost inflation. The 5% inclusionary reduction is a critical tailwind. Watch for similar density/height requests at Treasure Island, 655 Fourth Street, and One Oak—a pattern that suggests San Francisco's apartment supply recovery depends on regulatory concessions (reduced affordability, increased height) that offset construction cost headwinds. For competing Bay Area developers: expect zoning/affordability volatility; longer entitlement timelines as city evaluates cumulative density; and potential ratcheting-back of concessions if affordability advocates push back.

stories.title: Sterling Bay plans to offload Fulton Market site to Range Group for 109-unit mid-rise project
stories.excerpt: Fulton Market could be getting a new 109-unit mid-rise apartment building if the city approves the developer’s zoning application.  A company tied to Chicago-based Range Group put in a zoning application to build a five-story apartment complex at a lot the firm is under contract to purchase from Sterling Bay, according to Crain’s. The lot at 113 West Kinzie Street spans 27,714 square feet, and is one of three properties that Chicago-based Sterling Bay is offloading that are set for residential redevelopment.  The proposal includes specifications to align the building with Fulton Market’s affordability requirements. 15 percent of the 109 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Range Group acquires Sterling Bay's Fulton Market site (113 W Kinzie): 109-unit, 5-story mid-rise, 27.7K SF lot.
15% affordable units + fee-in-lieu for remaining 5% to hit 20% city requirement.
Fulton Market resilience: smaller projects outperform large-scale pipeline; infrastructure strain + historic preservation favor density discipline.
stories.summary: Range Group is under contract to acquire a 27,714-square-foot lot from Sterling Bay at 113 West Kinzie in Chicago's Fulton Market, with plans to develop a 109-unit, five-story mid-rise. The project will include 15% affordable units and a fee-in-lieu payment to meet the city's 20% affordability target. Sterling Bay is offloading three parcels in Fulton Market earmarked for residential redevelopment.
stories.impact: Fulton Market's strength despite downtown Chicago office weakness stems from smaller infill projects that respect historic preservation and avoid infrastructure strain—a model that outperforms large-scale speculative development. Range Group's acquisition signals continued confidence in the district's fundamentals (walkability, mixed-use appeal, restaurant/retail gravity). For developers: avoid Fulton Market megaprojects; smaller, contextual mid-rises (100–300 units) with strong ground-floor activation are the winning playbook. The district's absorption is stable; rents remain resilient vs. downtown proper, validating the submarkets as a defensive alternative to struggling Loop/South Loop markets.

stories.title: Tishman Speyer Hires Former Prologis Exec Joseph Ghazal to Lead International Growth
stories.excerpt: Tishman Speyer has hired former Prologis executive Joseph Ghazal as its new senior managing director and global head of growth, the firm announced Thursday. Ghazal, formerly global chief investment officer at Prologis, will lead Tishman’s international growth strategy and expand the firm’s presence into the Middle East and India by identifying acquisition and development opportunities in those markets. “This is an exceptional opportunity to build upon one of the great foundations in global real estate,” Ghazal said in a statement. “I look forward to partnering with colleagues across the globe and to leveraging my international experience to further expand Tishman Speyer’s impact and reach.” During his more than two decades at Prologis, Ghazal held several senior leadership positions across Europe, the Middle East, Asia and the Americas. In his most recent role there as global chief investment officer, he oversaw investment strategy, capital deployment and business development. A spokesperson for Prologis said the company is “grateful for Joseph’s contributions and wish him well in his next chapter.” In his new position at Tishman, Ghazal will be based in New York City and report directly to CEO Rob Speyer. He will start in August. “Over the last decade, Tishman Speyer has intentionally and successfully doubled down on our growth and diversification strategy. We have entered new international markets, launched a range of investment vehicles and created new product lines,” Speyer said in a statement. “As someone who has spent his career doing exactly this, Joseph is perfectly positioned to help spearhead our next era of growth.” Tishman has $70 billion in assets under management across 40 markets in the U.S., Europe, Asia and Latin America. With Ghazal’s help, the firm plans to expand into several new markets. But that’s not to say the firm hasn’t been busy at its headquarters in New York City. Tishman sealed $214 million of construction financing to
bullets: Tishman Speyer hires Joseph Ghazal (ex-Prologis Global CIO) as Senior MD, Global Head of Growth.
Mandate: expand Middle East & India; identify acquisition and development opportunities in high-growth emerging markets.
Tishman internationalizes portfolio; Ghazal's 20+ years Prologis industrial experience signals real-estate-as-capital-allocator play.
stories.summary: Tishman Speyer appointed Joseph Ghazal, former global chief investment officer at Prologis, as senior managing director and global head of growth. Ghazal will lead the firm's international expansion strategy, focusing on acquisition and development opportunities in the Middle East and India. He brings more than two decades of Prologis experience across Europe, Middle East, Asia, and the Americas.
stories.impact: Limited near-term multifamily impact for U.S. developers. However, Ghazal's hiring signals that institutional mega-cap developers (Tishman Speyer) are rotating capital toward emerging-market real estate, potentially reducing competitive pressure and capital competition in U.S. multifamily markets. Watch whether other Tier-1 developers follow suit with international pivots; if capital rotation accelerates, it could ease underwriting and financing conditions for domestic multifamily development.

stories.title: Nuveen Green Capital Supplies $101M C-PACE Loan for Newark Studios
stories.excerpt: Lights, camera, action! Great Point Studios has sealed $101 million of construction financing to develop a film and TV production studio campus in Newark, N.J., Commercial Observer has learned. Nuveen Green Capital originated the Commercial Property Assessed Clean Energy (C-PACE) loan for the Great Point Studios Newark project to be anchored by Lionsgate. The 270,000-square-foot development, which will have six soundstages and is 100 percent pre-leased, is slated for delivery in June 2027. “We are thrilled to partner with MCCP to capitalize this significant project, which marks not one, but two milestones, as the largest C-PACE transaction in New Jersey and the first in the city of Newark,” said Mike Doty, Senior Director of Originations, Nuveen Green Capital. “Through C-PACE, the sponsor will realize meaningful cost savings by financing this project at a competitive rate over a longer term, preserving capital that can be redeployed into the development itself,” Mike Doty, Senior Director of Originations at Nuveen Green Capital, said in a statement. “As C-PACE continues to expand across asset classes and use cases, it is exciting to see it applied to a project of this scale and cultural significance, one that is strategically located close to New York City and will bring jobs, production activity, and sustainable infrastructure to Newark for years to come.” CBRE negotiated the C-PACE debt with a team consisting of Tom Traynor, Tom Rugg, William Moyer, Mark Fluent, David Milestone, Anthony Jasenski and Henry Fenmore. Great Point Studios Newark marks the first purpose-built film and TV studio in New Jersey created through tax credits. It is one of five major studio projects in the works throughout New Jersey as part of the state’s increased push to attract more film projects from Hollywood through tax incentives. “This financing is an important milestone that allows us to bring a world-class, purpose-built studio to a city with tremendous potential,”
bullets: Great Point Studios Newark secures $101M C-PACE financing from Nuveen Green Capital; largest NJ PACE deal.
270K SF, 6 soundstages, 100% pre-leased (Lionsgate anchor); delivery June 2027.
PACE financing unlocks entertainment/media production infrastructure; Newark's industrial/real-estate asset base signals emerging sector anchor.
stories.summary: Great Point Studios secured $101 million in Commercial Property Assessed Clean Energy (C-PACE) financing from Nuveen Green Capital for a 270,000-square-foot film and TV production studio campus in Newark, New Jersey. The deal is the largest C-PACE transaction in New Jersey and the first in Newark. The 100%-pre-leased project (anchored by Lionsgate) will feature six soundstages and is scheduled for delivery in June 2027.
stories.impact: Great Point Studios' C-PACE deal and pre-lease demonstrates Newark's emergence as a media/entertainment-production hub, which attracts creative workforce and supporting service employment. For multifamily developers: media production facilities drive demand for live-work apartments, creative-class housing, and mixed-use residential near studio/production clusters. Monitor Newark's entertainment-sector growth; if studio anchors accumulate, the metro could see justified residential density and rent growth in adjacent Ironbound, downtown, and waterfront submarkets. C-PACE availability also signals institutional willingness to backstop large non-traditional uses, which can enable creative mixed-use residential-entertainment developments.

stories.title: Mansfield taps Bridgeview for downtown renovation project after dropping Mike Hoque’s firm
stories.excerpt: The southeast Dallas suburb of Mansfield lined up a new developer for a long-awaited $50 million mixed-use project meant to energize its downtown area.  Mansfield City Council voted to greenlight a deal with Dallas-based Bridgeview for a planned 253 apartment units, 13,000 square feet earmarked for retail and commercial space and 7,800 square feet of public space at the northeast corner of Smith and Elm streets, according to the Dallas Business Journal.  The city has been eyeing redevelopment of the site since 2022. Previously, they had a deal with Dallas-based Hoque Global for the same project before Hoque missed a […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Mansfield, TX: Bridgeview replaces Hoque Global for downtown mixed-use; 253 apts, 13K SF retail, $50M project.
Hoque missed 2024 deadline (infrastructure, cost escalation); Bridgeview to deliver by 2027 on 4-acre site.
Tax increment reinvestment zone grants $5.5M over 15 years; reflects entitlement/cost complexity in suburban DFW redevelopment.
stories.summary: Mansfield City Council approved a deal with Dallas-based Bridgeview to develop a $50 million mixed-use downtown project featuring 253 apartment units, 13,000 square feet of retail/commercial space, and 7,800 square feet of public space at Smith and Elm streets. Bridgeview replaces Hoque Global, which missed a 2024 deadline due to infrastructure issues and cost escalation. The city will grant a $5.5 million tax increment reinvestment zone incentive over 15 years; Bridgeview must acquire land within five months and begin construction by August 2027.
stories.impact: Hoque's repeated deadline misses across Dallas-Fort Worth (University Hills extensions, Evans/Rosedale cancellations) signal material underestimation of suburban infrastructure constraints and cost escalation during the pandemic/post-pandemic cycle. Bridgeview's reboot with tighter timelines and municipal incentives reflects both market discipline and developer risk-aversion. For competing developers in DFW: suburban infill redevelopment timelines are stretching 2–3 years beyond historical norms; infrastructure (utilities, traffic, drainage) is the binding constraint. Budget accordingly; pursue tax incentives aggressively; negotiate contingencies around force majeure and infrastructure delays.

stories.title: Ralph Zucker’s luxury condo development in Asbury Park snags $186M loan
stories.excerpt: The developer behind the iconic Bell Works revitalization in Chicago secured a massive construction loan for a banner condo project on the Jersey Shore. Zucker’s Inspired by Somerset Development snagged a $186 million loan from Madison Realty Capital for the development located at 1201 Ocean Avenue North in Asbury Park, New Jersey.  Meridian Capital Group’s David Bollag and James Darling arranged the financing for Zucker’s Inspired by Somerset Development, the real estate advisory firm announced Wednesday. The construction loan will fund the vertical development of the project. Lido Asbury Park, a rising on Asbury Park’s beachfront promenade, previously claimed a […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Ralph Zucker's Inspired by Somerset Development secures $186M loan (Madison Realty Capital) for Asbury Park luxury condos.
Lido Asbury Park: 112 for-sale units (1–4 BR), retail, pool/spa/fitness; $7.6M penthouse set NJ record (July 2025).
Luxury condo presales and financing availability signal coastal NJ residential market recovery and high-net-worth demand.
stories.summary: Inspired by Somerset Development, led by developer Ralph Zucker (Bell Works Chicago), secured a $186 million construction loan from Madison Realty Capital for Lido Asbury Park, a luxury for-sale condominium project at 1201 Ocean Avenue North in Asbury Park, New Jersey. The 112-unit project (one to four bedrooms) includes ground-floor retail, pool, wellness spa, fitness center, and landscaped park; completion is expected in 2027. In July 2025, a penthouse sold for $7.6 million, setting a New Jersey condo record.
stories.impact: Zucker's successful capital raise and strong presales ($7.6M penthouse) validate luxury coastal New Jersey residential as institutional-financing-worthy, signaling capital confidence in high-net-worth demand and oceanfront scarcity. For multifamily developers: the for-sale condo success in Asbury Park (vs. rental weakness in many coastal markets) suggests that high-end oceanfront goes ownership-first in current capital environment. However, watch for spillover demand into rental stock (workforce housing, second-market renters priced out of condos); smaller, well-positioned rental communities near Asbury Park's revitalization may capture affluent renter flow.

stories.title: Miami secures $193M permit to build civic center at Freedom Park
stories.excerpt: The city of Miami’s new civic center, which will replace an aging administrative building downtown and the city’s iconic city hall in Coconut Grove, secured a $193 million construction permit, the city confirmed. The city received its permit July 1,  just over a week before construction workers topped off the eight-story, 257,000-square-foot building on July 10. Adler Development is the developer, and Moss Construction is the general contractor.  The building will be part of the 130-acre Miami Freedom Park development, which is a $1 billion project anchored by Inter Miami CF’s stadium near Miami International Airport. The project, which includes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami Freedom Park civic center: $193M permit, 257K SF, 8 stories; topped out July 10; completion spring 2028.
Adler Development/Moss Construction; includes $20M pedestrian bridge connecting Metrorail; relocates City Hall from 1931 landmark.
130-acre $1B mixed-use anchored by Inter Miami CF stadium; civic center consolidation signals downtown-adjacent real-estate value migration.
stories.summary: Miami's new civic center at Freedom Park secured a $193 million construction permit and topped out July 10. The eight-story, 257,000-square-foot building (developed by Adler Development, general contractor Moss Construction) will consolidate city administrative functions from downtown and Coconut Grove, ending 70+ years at the iconic 1931 Pan Am building. A $20 million pedestrian bridge to the Metrorail station is under construction; the civic center is expected to open spring 2028 as part of the $1 billion, 130-acre Miami Freedom Park mixed-use development.
stories.impact: Miami Freedom Park's civic center as anchor signals a major southward/westward shift in Miami's civic and employment gravity away from downtown toward the airport corridor. This represents a significant demographic and employment-concentration move that will reshape residential demand patterns: expect rental demand to shift from Brickell/downtown toward Wynwood, Buena Vista, and airport-proximate submarkets. The $1B Freedom Park project (anchored by Inter Miami CF stadium) is a destination draw for hospitality and mixed-use tenants; nearby multifamily will capture worker and visitor demand. Developers should position new apartments and rentals near Freedom Park transit (Metrorail bridge access) and stadium-adjacent sites to capture the decade-long absorption wave.
